attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old dentist reluctantly presents to his General Practitioner at the request of his partner due to chronic ringing in his both ears. He states that 'he can usually hear others talking, but find it difficult to understand conversations' especially in crowded rooms. He states the tinnitus is mild and does not trouble him, being most noticeable at night whilst lying in bed, but is not noticeable during the day. He is keen to avoid any long-term medical interventions if possible.

His history is significant for a recent mild chest infection which is currently being treated with a course of cephalexin, but is otherwise well and takes no other regular medications.

On otoscopic examination his external ear canal is not erythematous or inflamed and his tympanic membrane is clear with a normal light reflex. Rinne's test reveals air conduction is greater than bone conduction in both ears, whilst on Weber's test the sound of the tuning fork is heard in the centre of his forehead.

An audiogram conducted is significant for bilateral notching in both air and bone conduction between 3000-4000Hz.
attributes.stemQuestion: Given the likely diagnosis, which one of the following is the most appropriate first step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's symptoms, otoscopic examination, and audiogram results demonstrate a bilateral noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL), which is most often experienced as hearing loss between 3000-4000Hz (relatively high frequencies). The most likely cause in this case is due to repeated exposure to a dental drill. Noise exposure hearing loss leads to bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, affecting higher frequencies first, and inducing tinnitus. Commonly, tinnitus can be worse at night due to a decrease in the absence of other environmental sounds (i.e. when in a quiet bedroom).

Patients with NIHL often find it difficult to comprehend speech, as the majority of speech information is carried by consonants that are of high frequencies. This can be especially exacerbated in an environment with high ambient noise, as consonants are also soft, leading to patients having difficulty in understanding as speech sounds become muffled.

Hearing protection is the most appropriate initial management option for this patient in order to prevent further hearing damage. Although some damage has already occurred, preventing further changes is vital to appropriate management. All patients unable to escape exposure to loud noises should have hearing protection regardless of disease progression. It should be noted that the patients symptoms do not appear to be troubling him and he is keen to avoid treatment, therefore do not need to be urgently managed.

Cease the cephalexin: Certain antibiotics do cause medication-induced hearing loss, most commonly aminoglycosides, macrolides and quinolones. However, cephalosporins such has cephalexin have not been shown to be ototoxic.

Hearing aids can be an effective treatment for both hearing loss and tinnitus. They amplify sounds entering the tympanic membrane, making them easier to hear. For patients experiencing tinnitus, amplifying external sounds can reduce the relative noise of tinnitus, making it less noticeable. However, this patient does not require a hearing aid as his symptoms do not seem to trouble him and he has preserved hearing at all other frequencies. In the future, particularly if symptoms progress, he would likely require hearing aids.

Cochlear implants are surgically implanted prosthetic devices that use electrical stimulation to provide hearing. They are usually reserved for patients with moderate to severe sensorineural hearing loss and after an unsuccessful trial of hearing aids due to the increased invasiveness associated with implantation.

Intratympanic steroids can be an effective treatment for sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL), including those with an identifiable cause (e.g. viral cochleitis, ototoxic drugs, neoplasms, trauma and Meniere disease) and those with a presumed idiopathic aetiology. They are not a long-term management solution.
learningPoints: Prolonged exposure to loud noise results in sensorineural hearing loss primarily impacting the relatively higher frequencies (between 3000-4000 Hz).
references: Nair C. Noise-induced hearing loss. InnovAiT [Internet]. 2014 Apr [cited 2023 Aug 11];7(4):204-8. Available from: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1177/1755738013518903?journalCode=inoa doi: 10.1177/1755738013518903

Ding T, Yan A, Liu K. What is noise-induced hearing loss?. Br J Hosp Med (Lond) [Internet]. 2019 Sep 2 [cited 2023 Aug 11];80(9):525-9. Available from: https://www.magonlinelibrary.com/doi/full/10.12968/hmed.2019.80.9.525
